remarks.title: Strengthening Africa’s Geopolitical Agency and Trade Resilience in a Multipolar World

speaker.name: Hanan Morsy
speaker.title: Deputy Executive Secretary and Chief Economist
speaker.organization: United Nations Economic Commission for Africa

summary.executive_summary: Hanan Morsy framed the conference as a timely forum for African researchers, practitioners, experts, and policymakers to address the continent’s challenges amid profound global economic and geopolitical change. She highlighted slower growth, geopolitical tensions, shifting trade alliances, technological disruption, climate change, tighter fiscal conditions, and the spillovers of conflict in the Middle East. These pressures create significant risks for Africa, but also an opportunity to increase its influence and shape global change. Morsy emphasized Africa’s strengths: projected growth above 4% in 2026, 22 economies expected to grow by more than 5%, a 1.5-billion-person continental market under the AfCFTA, abundant critical minerals, and the world’s largest labor force by 2035. She cautioned, however, that resources and demographic growth will not produce prosperity without value addition, productive employment, competitive industries, strong institutions, and regional coordination. She proposed five priorities: accelerate industrialization and regional value chains; implement the AfCFTA; mobilize domestic resources and deepen capital markets; invest in climate, energy, digital, food, and macroeconomic resilience; and strengthen Africa’s collective voice in global economic governance. She concluded that Africa should not only withstand global shocks but help shape the next era of global growth.
summary.purpose: To frame the conference’s central theme, assess Africa’s risks and strategic advantages, and present a five-point agenda for stronger resilience, structural transformation, and geopolitical influence.

main_themes.description: Slower growth, geopolitical tensions, changing trade alliances, technology, climate change, and fiscal pressure are reshaping global economic relations.
Growth momentum, the AfCFTA market, critical minerals, and a rapidly expanding labor force give Africa significant strategic advantages.
Natural resources, labor, and markets must be converted into productive employment, competitive industries, and higher-value regional production.
The AfCFTA and regional value chains can reduce dependence on external markets, strengthen supply chains, and increase Africa’s bargaining power.

main_themes.theme: Domestic finance and long-term economic security
main_themes.description: Domestic resource mobilization, local capital markets, climate resilience, energy security, digital infrastructure, food systems, and macroeconomic stability should be pursued together.
Africa’s geopolitical agency depends on stronger continental institutions, coordinated policies, sustained partnerships, and a unified voice in global governance.

key_messages.message: Africa should position itself not only to respond to global change, but to influence its direction.
Africa’s strategic assets will not generate prosperity without value addition, productive jobs, competitive industries, and capable institutions.
Industrialization and regional value chains are essential for retaining value, creating jobs, and reducing vulnerability to external shocks.
The AfCFTA must be implemented through infrastructure, efficient borders, harmonized regulations, digital trade systems, and competitive logistics.
Africa must finance more of its ambitions through domestic resources, local-currency capital markets, and institutional investors.
Africa’s influence will be greatest when it speaks with one voice in global economic governance.
The objective is not merely to survive global uncertainty, but to help define the next era of global growth.

priorities_and_challenges: Managing the effects of geopolitical tensions, conflict, inflation, disrupted supply chains, and tighter financing conditions.
Moving beyond raw-material exports toward higher-value production.
Creating productive employment for Africa’s expanding labor force.
Accelerating implementation of the African Continental Free Trade Area.
Mobilizing domestic resources and reducing dependence on external financing.
Strengthening Africa’s collective voice in global economic governance.
commitments_and_announcements: The United Nations Economic Commission for Africa reaffirmed its commitment to work with partners to turn conference dialogue into concrete action.
The speaker presented five priorities to guide collective African action.
policy_implications: Industrial policy should support regional value chains in critical minerals, pharmaceuticals, agriculture, textiles, and green manufacturing.
AfCFTA implementation should be backed by transport corridors, efficient borders, harmonized regulations, digital systems, and logistics.
Governments should strengthen tax administration and reduce illicit financial flows.
Local-currency capital markets and institutional investors should play a larger role in financing development.
Climate, energy, food, digital, and macroeconomic resilience should be treated as an integrated policy agenda.
African countries should coordinate positions on debt, climate finance, taxation, trade rules, and global financial reform.
calls_to_action: Accelerate industrialization and expand regional value chains.
Move decisively beyond exporting raw materials and commodities.
Accelerate implementation of the African Continental Free Trade Area.
Mobilize domestic resources and deepen domestic capital markets.
Invest more aggressively in long-term economic security.
Speak with greater unity and purpose in global economic governance.
partnerships_and_stakeholders: United Nations Economic Commission for Africa
Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development
summary.concluding_message: Africa should use the current period of global transformation as a catalyst for structural change. By trading more internally, financing more of its own development, processing more of its resources, investing in its people, and speaking with greater unity, the continent can shape rather than merely endure the next era of global growth.

powerful_quotes.quote: Our interest therefore must not be merely how Africa responds to change, but how it positions itself to influence the direction of that change.
powerful_quotes.context: Framing Africa’s goal as active influence rather than passive adaptation.

powerful_quotes.quote: Assets alone will not create the prosperity we seek.
powerful_quotes.context: Warning that natural resources, demographics, and market size require productive transformation.

powerful_quotes.quote: Africa's influence will be greatest when it speaks in one voice.
powerful_quotes.context: Calling for collective African positions in global economic governance.

powerful_quotes.quote: This is one of those moments.
powerful_quotes.context: Describing the rare alignment between global transformation and continental opportunity.

powerful_quotes.quote: It will help define the next era of global growth.
powerful_quotes.context: Expressing the concluding vision of a more integrated, self-financing, productive, and united Africa.

highlights.text: Africa’s economy is projected to grow by more than 4% in 2026, with 22 economies expected to expand by over 5%.
The AfCFTA represents a market of 1.5 billion people with combined GDP exceeding $3.4 trillion.
Africa possesses more than half of global critical-mineral resources and is expected to have the world’s largest labor force by 2035.
Five priorities were proposed: industrialization, AfCFTA implementation, domestic finance, long-term resilience, and a unified African voice.
Morocco, Côte d’Ivoire, Egypt, and Kenya were cited as examples of manufacturing, processing, renewable-energy, logistics, and digital progress.
The Economic Commission for Africa committed to converting the conference dialogue into concrete action for shared prosperity.

uncertain_items: Several names in the opening acknowledgements are mistranscribed, including the names of senior officials.
The transcript repeatedly renders 'multipolar world' as 'multi-border world'; the intended conference theme was normalized.
The phrase near 00:10:10 appears to mean that Africa cannot finance its ambitions primarily through external sources, but one word is unclear.
The phrase 'efficient orders' near 00:09:20 was interpreted as 'efficient borders' based on context.
The location and exact date are not stated in the transcript.
